name: Water
description: Universal solvent essential for life. Exhibits hydrogen bonding giving it anomalously high boiling point.

name: Sodium Chloride
description: Common table salt; classic example of ionic bonding with rock-salt crystal structure.

name: Hydrochloric Acid
description: Strong acid; hydrogen chloride gas dissolves in water to form hydrochloric acid.

name: Sulfuric Acid

hazards.notes: Highly corrosive; exothermic reaction with water

description: Strong diprotic acid; one of the most important industrial chemicals.

name: Nitric Acid
description: Strong acid and powerful oxidizer used in fertilizer production and explosives.

name: Phosphoric Acid
description: Triprotic acid used in fertilizers, food flavoring (cola), and rust removal.

name: Sodium Hydroxide
description: Strong base widely used in manufacturing, soap making, and drain cleaning.

name: Potassium Hydroxide
description: Strong base used in soap making and as an electrolyte in alkaline batteries.

name: Ammonia

hazards.notes: Toxic gas; corrosive to mucous membranes

description: Pyramidal molecule with lone pair. Essential for fertilizers; pungent odor.

name: Carbon Dioxide
description: Linear molecule; key greenhouse gas. Sublimes at 1 atm (no liquid phase at standard pressure).

name: Carbon Monoxide
description: Colorless, odorless toxic gas produced by incomplete combustion.

name: Hydrogen Peroxide

hazards.notes: Strong oxidizer; concentrated forms can cause burns

description: Strong oxidizer used as disinfectant and bleaching agent. Decomposes to water and oxygen.

name: Hydrogen Sulfide

hazards.notes: Highly toxic; heavier than air; deadens sense of smell

description: Toxic gas with characteristic rotten egg odor. Found in volcanic gases and hot springs.

name: Hydrogen Fluoride

hazards.notes: Extremely dangerous; penetrates skin causing deep burns

description: Extremely corrosive gas. Aqueous solution etches glass and penetrates skin.

name: Methane
description: Simplest alkane; tetrahedral geometry. Primary component of natural gas.

name: Ethane
description: Second simplest alkane. Component of natural gas.

name: Propane
description: Three-carbon alkane used as fuel (LPG) and refrigerant.

name: Butane
description: Four-carbon alkane commonly used in lighters and as a fuel gas.

name: Pentane
description: Five-carbon alkane; first liquid alkane at STP. Used as laboratory solvent.

name: Hexane
description: Six-carbon alkane widely used as non-polar solvent.

name: Octane
description: Eight-carbon alkane; reference standard for gasoline octane rating.

name: Decane
description: Ten-carbon alkane; component of diesel fuel and kerosene.

name: Ethylene
description: Simplest alkene with C=C double bond. Most produced organic compound worldwide; used to make polyethylene.

name: Acetylene

phase_notes.liquid: Sublimes at 1 atm; liquid only under pressure

hazards.notes: Extremely flammable; can explode under pressure

description: Simplest alkyne with C≡C triple bond. Used in welding and organic synthesis.

name: Benzene
description: Prototype aromatic compound; planar hexagonal ring with delocalized pi electrons.

name: Toluene
description: Methylbenzene; common industrial solvent and precursor to TNT.

name: Methanol

hazards.notes: Toxic; ingestion causes blindness and death

description: Simplest alcohol. Toxic if ingested; used as solvent and fuel.

name: Ethanol
description: Drinking alcohol; produced by fermentation. Demonstrates hydrogen bonding.

name: Acetic Acid
description: Weak acid; main component of vinegar. Forms dimers through hydrogen bonding.

name: Formic Acid
description: Simplest carboxylic acid; found in ant venom. Used in leather tanning.

name: Acetone

hazards.notes: Highly flammable; CNS depressant in high concentrations

description: Simplest ketone; widely used solvent. Miscible with water.

name: Formaldehyde
description: Simplest aldehyde. Preservative (formalin); used in resin production. Carcinogenic.

name: Glucose
description: Primary energy source for cells. Decomposes before boiling.

name: Sucrose
description: Common table sugar; disaccharide of glucose and fructose. Decomposes (caramelizes) before boiling.

name: Urea
description: Primary nitrogen-containing waste product in mammals. Key fertilizer compound.

name: Glycine
description: Simplest amino acid; building block of proteins. Decomposes before boiling.

name: Silicon Dioxide
description: Most abundant mineral in Earth's crust. Network covalent solid; used in glass and electronics.

name: Aluminum Oxide
description: Extremely hard ceramic. Corundum structure; sapphire and ruby are colored varieties.

name: Iron(III) Oxide
description: Common iron ore (hematite). Responsible for rust's red-brown color.

name: Titanium Dioxide
description: Widely used white pigment and photocatalyst. High refractive index.

name: Copper(II) Oxide
description: Black copper oxide used in ceramics and as semiconductor material.

name: Zinc Oxide
description: Wide band-gap semiconductor used in sunscreen and rubber vulcanization.

name: Magnesium Oxide
description: Refractory ceramic with very high melting point. Used in furnace linings.

name: Calcium Oxide
description: Quicklime; produced by calcination of limestone. Reacts exothermically with water.

name: Sulfur Dioxide
description: Toxic gas responsible for acid rain. Used as food preservative and bleaching agent.

name: Sulfur Trioxide
description: Highly reactive oxide; precursor to sulfuric acid.

name: Nitrogen Dioxide
description: Reddish-brown toxic gas and major air pollutant. Precursor to acid rain.

name: Dinitrogen Monoxide
description: Nitrous oxide; used as anesthetic and in whipped cream aerosol. Potent greenhouse gas.

name: Nitric Oxide
description: Signaling molecule in biology. Free radical; reacts readily with oxygen.

name: Calcium Carbonate
description: Major component of limestone, chalk, and marble. Shells and coral are largely CaCO3.

name: Sodium Carbonate
description: Common cleaning agent and water softener. Used in glass manufacturing.

name: Sodium Bicarbonate
description: Baking soda; leavening agent. Decomposes to Na2CO3, H2O, and CO2 when heated.

name: Potassium Chloride
description: Common potassium salt used as fertilizer and salt substitute.

name: Calcium Sulfate
description: Gypsum; used in plaster, drywall, and cement. Dihydrate form is plaster of Paris.

name: Sodium Sulfate
description: Used in detergents, paper pulping, and glass manufacturing.

name: Calcium Fluoride
description: Fluorite; source of fluorine. UV-transparent crystal used in optics.

name: Barium Sulfate
description: Used as radio-contrast agent (barium meal) and in drilling muds.

name: Silver Nitrate
description: Key reagent in analytical chemistry; precursor to photographic silver halides.

name: Potassium Permanganate
description: Strong oxidizing agent; purple solution used in water treatment and analytical chemistry.

name: Sodium Fluoride
description: Used in toothpaste and water fluoridation. Toxic in large amounts.

name: Potassium Iodide
description: Used to protect thyroid from radioactive iodine; food additive for iodine supplementation.

name: Calcium Chloride
description: Hygroscopic salt used for de-icing roads and as desiccant.

name: Lithium Fluoride
description: Highest electronegativity difference; used in UV optics and nuclear applications.

name: Magnesium Chloride
description: Common in seawater; used in dust control and as de-icer.

name: Silicon Carbide
description: Extremely hard ceramic semiconductor. Used in abrasives and high-power electronics.

name: Boron Nitride
description: Isoelectronic with carbon; hexagonal form is a lubricant, cubic form is ultra-hard.

name: Zirconium Dioxide
description: High-temperature ceramic; cubic form used as diamond simulant (cubic zirconia).

name: Gallium Arsenide
description: Direct band gap semiconductor used in LEDs, lasers, and solar cells.

name: Gallium Nitride
description: Wide band gap semiconductor for blue LEDs and power electronics.

name: Tungsten Carbide
description: Extremely hard material used in cutting tools and drill bits.

name: Diamond
description: Hardest natural material. Pure carbon in tetrahedral sp3 coordination.

name: Graphite
description: Layered sp2 carbon; semimetal. Conducts within planes but not between them.

name: Steel (Carbon)
description: Iron-carbon alloy; most widely used structural metal. Carbon content 0.2-2.1%.

name: Stainless Steel 304
description: Austenitic stainless steel; corrosion-resistant alloy for kitchenware and medical instruments.

name: Brass
description: Copper-zinc alloy used in hardware, musical instruments, and decorative items.

name: Bronze
description: Copper-tin alloy; historically important for tools and weapons.

name: Solder (Tin-Lead)
description: Traditional tin-lead solder for electronics. Lead-free alternatives now preferred.

name: Nichrome
description: Nickel-chromium alloy used in heating elements and resistors.

name: Duralumin
description: Aluminum alloy with copper and magnesium; used in aircraft construction.

name: Air
description: Earth's atmosphere; mixture of nitrogen, oxygen, argon, and trace gases.

name: Seawater
description: Average ocean water with ~3.5% salinity. Freezing point depressed by dissolved salts.

name: Soda-Lime Glass
description: Most common type of glass; used in windows, bottles, and containers.

name: Borosilicate Glass
description: Heat-resistant glass (Pyrex); low thermal expansion. Used in labware and cookware.

name: Portland Cement
description: Hydraulic cement that sets and hardens with water. Basis of concrete.

name: Sodium Hypochlorite

hazards.notes: Corrosive; do not mix with acids or ammonia

description: Active ingredient in household bleach. Strong oxidizer and disinfectant.

name: Calcium Hydroxide
description: Slaked lime; used in mortar, plaster, and water treatment.

name: Ammonium Nitrate

hazards.notes: Explosive under confinement with heat; regulated in many jurisdictions

description: Common fertilizer; also used as explosive when mixed with fuel oil (ANFO).

name: Sodium Nitrate
description: Used in fertilizers, food preservation, and pyrotechnics.

name: Potassium Nitrate
description: Key ingredient in gunpowder. Used in fertilizers and food preservation.

name: Copper Sulfate
description: Blue crystalline salt used in agriculture, electroplating, and analytical chemistry.

name: Iron(II) Sulfate
description: Used as iron supplement, water treatment, and lawn conditioner.

name: Sodium Sulfide

hazards.notes: Releases toxic H2S on contact with acids or moisture

description: Strong reducing agent used in paper pulping and ore processing.

name: Aluminum Chloride
description: Lewis acid catalyst widely used in organic chemistry (Friedel-Crafts reactions).

name: Phosphorus Pentachloride

hazards.notes: Reacts violently with water releasing HCl

description: Chlorinating agent in organic synthesis. Trigonal bipyramidal geometry.

name: Diethyl Ether

hazards.notes: Extremely flammable; forms explosive peroxides on storage

description: Common laboratory solvent; historically used as anesthetic. Forms explosive peroxides.

name: Chloroform
description: Dense organic solvent; historically used as anesthetic. Possible carcinogen.

name: Dimethyl Sulfoxide

hazards.notes: Penetrates skin; can carry dissolved toxins into bloodstream

description: Powerful polar aprotic solvent; penetrates skin rapidly carrying dissolved substances.

name: Glycerol
description: Viscous liquid used in food, pharmaceuticals, and as nitroglycerin precursor.

name: Nitroglycerin

hazards.notes: Extremely shock-sensitive explosive; also used medically for angina

description: Powerful explosive and vasodilator (medical use). Basis of dynamite.

name: TNT
description: Standard explosive; reference for explosive yield measurement (tons of TNT).

name: Caffeine

hazards.notes: Psychoactive; toxic in very large doses

description: Psychoactive stimulant found in coffee, tea, and chocolate. Most widely consumed drug.

name: Aspirin
description: Common analgesic and anti-inflammatory. One of the most widely used medications.

name: Citric Acid
description: Key metabolic intermediate (Krebs cycle). Provides tartness in citrus fruits.

name: Ascorbic Acid
description: Vitamin C; essential nutrient preventing scurvy. Powerful antioxidant.

name: Alanine
description: Second simplest amino acid; one of the 20 standard proteinogenic amino acids.

name: Thymine
description: One of the four DNA nucleobases; pairs with adenine via two hydrogen bonds.

name: Adenine
description: Purine nucleobase in DNA and RNA; component of ATP.

name: Cholesterol
description: Essential lipid in cell membranes; precursor to steroid hormones and vitamin D.

name: Sodium Chlorate

hazards.notes: Strong oxidizer; can cause fires with combustibles

description: Strong oxidizer used in herbicides and paper bleaching.

name: Lithium Hydride

hazards.notes: Reacts violently with water releasing H2

description: Lightest ionic compound. Used in hydrogen storage and as reducing agent.

name: Sodium Hydride

hazards.notes: Reacts violently with water; fire hazard

description: Strong base and reducing agent. Reacts violently with water.

name: Calcium Hydride
description: Drying agent for organic solvents. Generates hydrogen gas with water.

name: Boron Trifluoride
description: Trigonal planar Lewis acid; important industrial catalyst.

name: Silicon Tetrachloride

hazards.notes: Fumes in moist air; releases HCl with water

description: Precursor to high-purity silicon and fumed silica.

name: Phosphine

hazards.notes: Extremely toxic; can ignite spontaneously in air

description: Extremely toxic gas used in semiconductor manufacturing. Pyramidal like ammonia.

name: Arsine

hazards.notes: One of the most toxic industrial gases

description: Extremely toxic gas used in semiconductor doping.

name: Silane
description: Silicon analogue of methane. Used in semiconductor CVD processes.

name: Ozone

hazards.notes: Toxic at ground level; powerful oxidizer

description: Triatomic oxygen; absorbs UV radiation in stratosphere. Strong oxidizer.

name: Hydrogen Cyanide

hazards.notes: Extremely toxic; lethal in small amounts; controlled

description: Extremely toxic compound with characteristic bitter almond odor.

name: Sulfur Hexafluoride

hazards.notes: Non-toxic but potent greenhouse gas (23,500x CO2)

description: Dense inert gas used as electrical insulator. Octahedral geometry. Potent greenhouse gas.

name: Xenon Difluoride
description: One of the first noble gas compounds synthesized. Linear molecule demonstrating hypervalent bonding.

name: Vacuum

hazards.notes: Exposure to hard vacuum causes ebullism and rapid asphyxiation

description: A region devoid of all matter. True perfect vacuum is unattainable; the best laboratory vacuums reach ~10⁻¹² Pa. Permittivity and permeability take their exact free-space values (ε₀, μ₀).

name: Interstellar Medium (ISM)

hazards.notes: Not survivable without full life-support; radiation exposure

description: The diffuse interstellar medium (ISM) between star systems. Approximately 1 atom per cm³, composed of ~90.8% atomic hydrogen and ~9.1% helium by number, with trace heavier elements. Temperature ranges from ~10 K in dense molecular clouds to ~10⁶ K in hot ionized regions.

name: Oort Cloud
formula: H₂O / CO₂ / SiO₂ / CO / NH₃ / CH₄

hazards.notes: Not survivable; ~4 K ambient temperature; extreme isolation

description: The Oort Cloud is a hypothesized spherical shell of icy bodies surrounding the solar system from ~2,000 to ~200,000 AU. Total mass is ~5 Earth-masses spread over ~10⁵⁰ m³, giving a volume filling factor of ~5×10⁻³⁰ — overwhelmingly vacuum. The sparse cometary nuclei ('dirty snowballs') are composed of ~45% water ice, ~25% silicate dust, ~10% CO₂ ice, ~8% CO ice, ~5% ammonia ice, ~4% methane ice, and ~3% hydrogen cyanide ice. Ambient temperature is ~4–10 K.

name: Neutron Degenerate Matter
description: Bulk neutron matter found in neutron-star cores. Density reaches ~4×10¹⁷ kg/m³ — a teaspoon weighs ~5.5 billion tonnes. Held together by gravity overcoming neutron degeneracy pressure.

name: Electron Degenerate Matter
description: Carbon-oxygen plasma supported against gravitational collapse by electron degeneracy pressure. Found in white-dwarf cores at densities of ~10⁶–10⁹ kg/m³. Ions form a Coulomb crystal lattice at low temperatures.

name: Quark-Gluon Plasma
description: A state of matter at extreme temperature and density where quarks and gluons are no longer confined within hadrons. Theorized to exist in the inner cores of the most massive neutron stars, and briefly created in heavy-ion collisions at CERN and RHIC. Density ~10¹⁸ kg/m³.

name: Nuclear Pasta
description: Exotic nuclear phases found in neutron-star inner crusts at densities of ~10¹⁴ kg/m³. Competing nuclear and Coulomb forces create shapes named after Italian pasta: gnocchi (spheres), spaghetti (rods), lasagna (sheets), anti-spaghetti (tubes), and anti-gnocchi (bubbles).

name: Metallic Hydrogen
description: Hydrogen compressed to a metallic state at pressures above ~400 GPa. Predicted to be a room-temperature superconductor. Found deep inside gas giants like Jupiter and Saturn where pressures exceed millions of atmospheres. Density ~600–1000 kg/m³.

name: Liquid Metallic Hydrogen
description: A liquid phase of metallic hydrogen found in gas-giant interiors at pressures of ~1–4 Mbar and temperatures of ~5000–20000 K. Conducts electricity and generates planetary magnetic fields through dynamo action. Density ~1000–1400 kg/m³.

name: Degenerate Helium
description: Helium compressed to extreme density by electron degeneracy pressure in the cores of red-giant stars. Accumulates until reaching the helium flash ignition temperature (~10⁸ K), triggering runaway triple-alpha fusion. Density ~10⁶ kg/m³.

name: Stellar Plasma
description: Hot, fully ionized hydrogen-helium gas typical of stellar interiors and atmospheres. Temperature ranges from ~5000 K (photospheres) to ~10⁷ K (cores). Density varies enormously from ~10⁻¹² kg/m³ in coronae to ~150,000 kg/m³ in solar cores.

name: Relativistic Jet Material
description: Ultra-relativistic plasma in astrophysical jets from black holes and pulsars. Composed of electron-positron pairs and photons moving at near light speed. Extremely low rest-mass density but immense kinetic energy density.

name: Ionized Hydrogen
description: Photoionized hydrogen gas found in HII regions (emission nebulae) surrounding hot O- and B-type stars. UV radiation strips electrons from hydrogen atoms; recombination produces the characteristic red Hα glow at 656.3 nm. Density ~10⁻¹⁸ kg/m³.

name: Molecular Hydrogen Cloud
description: Dense molecular hydrogen clouds in the interstellar medium. Sites of active star formation. Temperatures ~10–50 K, densities ~10²–10⁶ particles/cm³. Traced by CO emission since H₂ has no permanent dipole.

name: Interstellar Dust
description: Microscopic solid particles (0.01–1 μm) of silicate and carbonaceous grains in the interstellar medium. Causes reddening and extinction of starlight. Serves as a catalyst for H₂ formation and a building block for planetesimals. Effective density in nebulae ~10⁻¹⁵ kg/m³.

name: Supernova Ejecta
description: Expanding shell of heavy-element-enriched gas ejected by a supernova explosion. Contains nucleosynthesis products from the progenitor star: iron, silicon, oxygen, sulfur, and heavier r-process elements. Temperatures ~10⁶–10⁸ K near the shock front, cooling over millennia.
